name: 48 · Live Camera Detection (live feed → YOLO26 → detections + annotated WebRTC feed + recorded artifact)
description: Closes the live-viz loop: a REAL live camera feed flows in over a lossy data channel, a YOLO26 detector recognizes objects frame-by-frame, and the workflow streams back BOTH a structured detection stream AND a box-annotated video you watch in the same panel. Start{source, duration_s, fps} → camera (cv2.VideoCapture → per-frame JPEG over lossy `nats-latest` Data/Out `frames`) → detector (stream('frames') → YOLO26 + ByteTrack per frame → emit `detections` Control/Out + draw boxes and stream the annotated JPEG over Data/Out `annotated`) → summary (gather `detections` → per-class report) → End. This is the source-agnostic camera thesis made concrete. The Start `source` field is whatever `cv2.VideoCapture` accepts — a webcam index ("0"), an RTSP/HTTP camera URL, or a local video file path — so the SAME workflow runs against a laptop webcam, an IP camera, or (later) a Gazebo / Isaac Lab simulated robot camera. In production you have a real camera; in sim you swap one string. The bytes ride the out-of-band datastream transport — the net never sees the frames, only each channel's open + close. The `camera` step captures a bounded `duration_s` window at a target `fps` (a workflow run terminates, a live feed does not), JPEG-encodes each frame, and writes it as one self-contained chunk over the `frames` Data channel whose declared transport is `nats-latest` — lossy-latest core NATS. If the detector falls behind, OLD frames are dropped whole and the latest wins: exactly camera semantics, and lossless per frame because a JPEG is independently decodable. (LIVE-ONLY for a webcam: macOS gates camera access behind a TCC permission prompt attributed to the terminal/app that launched the dev stack — grant it under System Settings → Privacy & Security → Camera, or point `source` at an RTSP/HTTP URL or a file path.) The `detector` step drains `frames` and runs YOLO26 + ByteTrack PER FRAME as each lands (`model.track(frame, persist=True)` keeps stable track ids across the per-frame calls). It produces two things at once: the Control channel `detections` (one `{frame,label,confidence,bbox,track_id}` token per object, the durable structured record, folded by the consumer-edge `join: gather` in `summary`), and the Data channel `annotated` — the same frame with YOLO's boxes/labels/track-ids drawn on it (`res.plot()`), JPEG-encoded over a durable JetStream transport. No node consumes `annotated`; the instance Channels panel taps it and plays it as a LIVE MJPEG feed. To make that viewable, this demo ships alongside a new frontend render adapter: `planLiveRender` now classifies `image/jpeg` data channels as `kind: 'mjpeg'`, and `playMjpegStream` splits the tapped byte stream on JPEG EOI markers (FF D9), swapping each complete frame into an `` — so you watch frames + bounding boxes stream in real time, the presentation-side analog of the MSE video path (demo 46). Pure-cv2 throughout (no PyAV) to avoid the libav symbol clash. Run it live (run via the API, not `mekhan test` — the detector cold-installs ultralytics/torch + downloads yolo26n.pt, past the 60 s test cap): `mekhan run demos/48-live-camera-detection --start-tokens demos/48-live-camera-detection/sample-input.json`, where the bundled sample-input requests the first webcam ("0"). Then open the instance, find the detector's `annotated` channel in the Channels panel, and click Play live to watch the annotated feed; the per-frame detection log streams in the execution log and the summary folds the per-class report. Set `source` to a video file path for a deterministic, camera-free run. A `recorder` step closes the persistence loop. The `annotated` WebRTC channel is a presentation-only EGRESS sink — a browser subscribes to it, but no in-net node can read a `livekit` channel back. So the detector mirrors the SAME annotated JPEG frames onto a second durable `recording` channel (transport `jetstream`: lossless, ordered, replayable), and the `recorder` step drains it into an MP4 with `cv2.VideoWriter` and registers that file in the platform's file catalogue via the SDK `log_artifact(..., blocking=True)`. The `summary` (report) and `recorder` (artifact) branches converge at an AND-join (`mode: "all"`) before End, so the instance can't complete and tear the net down until the recording is flushed and catalogued. Net result: you watch the live annotated feed over WebRTC AND keep a durable, catalogued recording of exactly what was streamed. This is the data-plane → AI → control-plane loop closed end-to-end AND fed back to the operator as live viz plus a persisted artifact — the foundation for a simulation-camera / live-robot-feedback view in robotic workflows.
